Wizzed through this pleasant morning offering. For me it’s always a bath, I’m not a shower fan except on the very hottest days. Perhaps it’s because I spend so much time standing in the rain corralling various animals. Our temporary ovine guests have discovered how to force up the protective wiring to nibble at our delicate young trees, meaning the first job every day is reinforcing the fencing and persuading stubborn sheep to eat the grass. 

I prefer my gin and TONIC with lemon please. Cheers.

A few years ago, my baby brother, who is 59, used the expression "slaps" on an all-sibling text thread. We all dropped him as a contact on our phones and didn't speak to him for a year. Harsh medicine? Sure. But he thanked us later. "It was the only way I was going to learn," he said.

Anyone having issues syncing across apple devices lately? Have even updated and reinstalled but no luck. Took a screenshot of crossword on iPad at 13:00 min. Opened puzzle on iPhone and done in 4:32. Back to iPad, puzzle is locked. Closed and reopened there,  completion time is 4:32. Time travel?

Since I don't have anything specific to say about this puzzle, which was a worthy Tuesday effort, I'd like to focus on a complaint heard on Sunday.

If you haven't done Sunday's puzzle yet, and would rather not see anything related to it, turn away now.  I'll wait.





Still waiting....




OK, that should be good.

On Sunday, a lot of people complained that since they didn't participate in the activity suggested by the colorful symbols, they couldn't solve it, or didn't have a good time with the puzzle.  In other words, because the theme was unfamiliar to them, the experience was ruined.

Good solving should not be dependent on one's intimate knowledge of the theme or any of the clues and entries.  The skill of good solving is figuring out what you don't know, and filling in missing answers via the crosses.  

Being aware of the world around you, taking note of things that aren't in your sphere of interest, and generally being well-rounded are the keys to good solving.  Hating things so much that you refuse to even osmose well-known items works against you as a solver.  You don't have to like rap to know that "Lil" is part of many a rapper's name.

Which leads me to a puzzle many may not even be aware of, the monthly Bonus puzzle.  It's not on the app; you have to access it on the website.  (No idea why that's so.)  But how this fits here will come in a reply.

(If you haven't done the Bonus puzzle yet, and don't want spoilers, do it before opening the reply!)

The whole time I was solving, I was trying to imagine different, more creative clues for the given entries--why is LINK an [_Online_ connection]? Why IRATE [_Absolutely_ steaming]? That, and wondering whether there will be a youtubeable intersection (there is, below). IMHO, She-Ra was always a more interesting character than He-Man, as Xena was to Hercules. Separated at birth, they inhabited parallel universes, with the occasional crossover. It's interesting to compare the series' theme songs and music--the leitmotiv for He-Man has a strong, masculine* rhythm, with the accent on the final syllable: "he-MAN!"; while She-Ra's leitmotiv has a more lilting feminine* one: "SHE-ra, SHE-ra" (*I'm using "masculine" vs. "feminine" in their neutral, prosodic definitions of "weak-STRONG" versus "STRONG-weak," but the gender connotations are impossible to ignore.) I clearly had way too much time on my hands in the early 80's. *** *** The Karakoram are alive: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KVion9SrlJU&list=RDKVion9SrlJU&start_radio=1

Not a hard puzzle, but enough already with slang crossing slang, i.e. SPITS BARS and SLAPS, and "informally" i.e. NORMIE (65A: Person with mainstream likes, pejoratively), AMIRITE (23A: "Don't you agree with me?," informally). 

Yes, I do say and write "gonna" and "wanna," but, "gimme" a break, you're never gonna convince me that AMIRITE is anything but awful. As for NORMIE, I have a cousin Normie, but since he's from Brooklyn, as am I, he has always been called NAWMIE, even after we both lost our accents.

I've made cornbread in my Lodge cast iron for decades. Never knew it was called PONE. 

With my last 3 squares being SLAPS/SPITSBARS, LIESL/LINK, FROS/PONE, it was enough to invoke Check Puzzle and lose the gold.

Very sad performance on my part for a Tuesday. Cheers.

Cute, 
but way off today.

STIR.

Instantly regret plan.

Attempt to layer up to
face the chilly morning, 
Try to find the sleeves of 
a pillowcase
("What *is* this? 
Pants..?")

Try to hide the pre-caffeine 
hatred for everything including
lovely hubby as we trek up the hill
to see the blood moon.

Begin to feel human.

Overcast skies block it
but the sunrise was stunning.

Walk back to the house feeling grateful.

Sip tea with pups and blanket on my lap.

Do puzzle.


Morning all!

Quick!  What's the difference between `1) STIR THE POT, GET UP TO SPEED and FIRST THINGS FIRST and 2) SLAPS, as used here, and SPITS BARS?

Answer: The former have "legs", i.e. they've lasted.  The latter don't and probably won't.

For those who do puzzles from the archives and for me who has Will Shortz puzzle collections in hardcover, the main difference is the lack of ephemeral youthy slang in the old puzzles.  Ephemeral youthy slang means that these puzzles will probably be out of date by the time they are ready to be collected and published.  Meaning they may never be collected and published.

And it's too bad, because most of this puzzle has grown-up non-junky fill.

The biggest questions for me were:  Would the "image problem" be a BLUR or a SLUR?  And is "is awesome", in slang SLAPS, SLIPS or SLOPS?  One makes just about as much sense to me as the other.  YMMV

commentBody:

I'll stand up for the idiomaticity (is that a word?) of HUSHES UP. It's not an artificial lengthened version just to plug some grid holes. It's a separate idiom, including the UP.
That said, I found a lot of meh clues and fills here, and the theme likewise. Two honourable exceptions: MASALA which hasn't worn out its welcome yet; and  SLAPS, as clued in modern slang.

commentBody:
@Andrzej
Why do you erase your linguistic heritage, Polish?

@Andrzej
I don't think it's the phrase itself, so much is the spelling that's annoying. For me, anyway.

@NanuNanu

But that's exactly my point - on some level how you spell stuff in English is fluid. If the same phrase can appear in the crossword beginning with oh/ah/aw, why can't "am I right" be AMIRITE in peace?

@SBK

We don't. We don't deny where our words come from. We just agree our spelling should make sense in everyday communication, rather than serve heritage over utility

@Andrzej

For someone who spends so much of his time posting in English about an English-language game, you sure do seem to hate English. Really weird.

@Andrzej

When I learned some Czech a couple decades back, I was most pleasantly surprised to discover that the language’s writing conventions included a strictly deterministic mapping between spelling and pronunciation…and only years later did it occur to me to wonder if English was more of an exception or a norm, globally.

The answer, roughly, seems to be: an exception. There is a fair bit of interesting nuance in many cases (e.g. languages like Arabic, in which many/most vowels are omitted in common forms of writing), but yeah, with English it’s basically just a total crapshoot.

I remember a classroom exercise from back in first grade (~6-year olds), in which the class was presented with a list of words, and we made our way through that list by cycling through the students, each attempting to pronounce the current word until someone got it “right”.

The one word I can specifically remember from that list is “sought”; and as I recall, we made it through the entire class, with each student’s vocalization being deemed incorrect, until the teacher finally set us straight…with a pronunciation that sounded more or less indistinguishable from that of the students who had gone before him.

@Andrzej

This is a continuation of a thread from a few days ago, I think. I recognize that English spelling is often very irregular and to a degree arbitrary. There are usually linguistic precedents, for sure, but some academics at some point rigorized the spelling and made some decisions that don’t always make sense. And I really don’t have a big issue with casual spellings in texts, ads, etc. It’s natural. But I will say when you are reading you want the words to flow into your brain naturally and they don’t when standard spellings aren’t used. And it may reflect a bit of laziness or lack of education which like it or not reflects on the writer. So if my colleague were to send me an email—even a casual one—that says AM I RITE, it’s just going to rub me the wrong way. It’s just actually harder to read and it takes an extra neuron or two. That’s just what I mean. Of course people do write that way sometimes, so I’m bothered by the phrase itself not by the fact it was used as an entry.

@Andrzej

The answer is simple.  The Norman Conquest.

Before 1066, everyone was speaking Anglo-Saxon (or other local languages).  After 1066, most were still speaking those languages, but the nobility was speaking Norman French.  And those two languages had widely differing spelling rules.

Add in the fact that there were no dictionaries or printing presses, so when the languages began to merge into early English, people gave up trying to standardize the spelling.  What's more, pronunciation was so varied that matching the spelling to the pronunciation was dead on arrival.

Only one person since then has been able to rein in some of the spelling to match the pronunciation: the American Noah Webster.  He decided the u in labour and similar words was unnecessary; that words like centre and theatre are pronounced with the e before the r, so he switched the two, and that words ending in -ise, like civilise, sounded more like -ize, so he respelled those.

Americans adopted those changes, but the mother country made fun of us.  And they still do.  After all, they invented the language.

But English spelling is so far gone that there's no way to standardize--or standardise--it.

@Joe

Why hate? I have just found it weird for 40 years how English spells words. Spelling is much more straightforward in the other languages I speak.

I don't buy it, Steve. Why can us Poles agree how to spell thousands of words derived from Russian, English, French, German, Turkish, Czech, Hungarian, Italian, Tatar (etc.) but English speakers can't? We actually have a body (Rada Języka Polskiego, the Council of the Polish Langauge) which standardizes our spelling and grammar every few years - the latest updated rules, consistent with modern usage, came into force this year. It can be done. Stuff not being doable and nobody bothering to do stuff is not the same.

And, as always, I wouldn't be writing any of this if it were not for Americans often making fun of Polish because of how it's spelled - when what we're doing actually makes much more sense than the American way 🤷🏽‍♂️

@Andrzej English has a plethora of oddities, for sure! This is a fun post about them. I think it's fun, anyhow. I notice and chuckle about such oddities all the time. https://www.grammarly.com/blog/language-trends-culture/english-language-quirks/

@Andrzej

Because your language took all those loanwords in bit by bit.  When two languages clash like tectonic plates, weird things happen.

The reason we don't have a body like your Council, or the French and Spanish Academies, is obvious.  The English-speaking colonized lands, particularly the US and India, now have more speakers than the mother country.  So any Council or Academy would have limited effect on the development of the language.  Had it been created in the 1600's, perhaps, but then we'd all be talking like Shakespeare.

PS. Despite having their vaunted academy, les francais say "le weekend" anyway.

@Steve See also yesterday's discussion.

In rough descending order of popularity (according to me). Not exhaustive.

At home: Loo, Toilet, Bathroom (if there's a shower/bath), S*****r, P****r, Lavatory/Lav if you're 100 years old

In public venue: Toilet(s), Loo(s), Gents/Ladies, Bog(s), Little Boys' Room, WC, Lavatory/Lav if you're 100 years old

@Steve
The weirdness of the British class system:
Upper class - lavatory and never toilet (so common) but loo accepted now. So I've heard.
Working class (in the past) lav and WC and now toilet, loo, bog, The Ladies, the Gents in shops, airports etc.
As a chid in the 1950's we said 'the lavies'.

My grandsons are old enough now to want to use 'the boys' and not 'the girls' with me.

@Steve
Oddly, my public, American middle school/junior high in the mid-70's used a LAV pass when we pre-adolescents/teens needed to go to the restroom. Before paying attention to the word on the pass during our first week of school, most of us kept whispering to one another, "Why does everyone keep asking to go the the LAB?"

@Tim  When I was in second grade at a rural American school, I was taught to ask to go to the lavatory.  Of course, I am 64, so getting close to 100. I never did run into that word in America again after leaving elementary school.  I wonder what kids are taught today.
@Steve It depends on which words cross the second and third letters.
@Tim In the U.S. we also use the terms restroom, powder room, and half-bath, and probably a few others regionally.

@Al  in Pittsburgh

To quote James 3:2, true dat!!
@lucky13 Fraid so!
@lucky13 after making coffee

@lucky13
Not before coffee and mucking out the animals. If I’m lucky and it’s not raining so hard I have to grapple with the waterproofs, then I can usually hit the screen within an hour of STIRring.

@Ιασων

@Chris yes but it’s still not English 😀

@dutchiris

@dutchiris chatGPT would have liked it either way 😀😀

@bortney

@JayTee

@bortney File that one away for future reference…but not in deep storage. You will *definitely* be seeing it again: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Finder

@Steve L

The monthly Bonus puzzle debuts on the first of every month, and is available all month on the website Games page only.  It is a themed puzzle with a title.  The title clues you in, of course, on what the theme is, and the difference between daily puzzle themes and the Bonus puzzle's themes is that the daily themes are found in certain symmetrical theme answers, the Bonus puzzle tries to shoehorn the theme into as many answers as possible.

(Again, if you don't want spoilers, go find and do the Bonus puzzle and then come back.)








Anyway, I read those negative comments about the main puzzle on Sunday, and then opened the new monthly Bonus puzzle.  The title was "Get Real!", and the theme (not much of a spoiler, since a quick look at all three top-row across clues makes it obvious) is about reality shows.  (In fact, the puzzle was co-constructed by a reality star whom I've never heard of.)

This was as far from my wheelhouse as it got.  The closest I get to so-called reality shows is the nightly news, which is actual reality.

I did not recoil in horror.  I did not immediately refuse to do it.  What I did was get down to work.

The key is that crossword constructors do not want you to fail.  They cross unguessable names with things that can be figured out.  They make every crossword solvable.  They don't write clues like [My brother-in-law's middle name].

More to come in another reply.

@Steve L

The two longest across answers were unknowns to me, but many of the crosses were easy.  Some were guessable once some of their letters were revealed on crosses.  The long answer near the top (20A) could be figured out with only a few crosses; the lower one (52A) needed almost all of them, and even then, it was a guess.

I knew a few names just because I keep my eyes open; I've never watched "Dancing With the Stars," but I recognize the host's name (mostly because of his previous work); others were a total guess (like the "Drag Race" winner at 34A). 

That last one was an everyday name reclued to fit the theme; another one was a name I've seen before in puzzles which is usually clued as a state bird.  There's an actor who is clued as a reality game show host; I'd never seen the show, nor much of his other work, but his name still sounded familiar.

I don't want to go into a fourth installment, so I'll stop there, and say that I got to a solve in a reasonable time, despite very little interest in the theme.  It was a challenge to me to solve a puzzle out of my comfort zone, and it was just as much fun as most other puzzles (unless they're really in my zone).

Facing a challenge is what makes a puzzle fun.  It also expands one's knowledge base, even if that info is trivia.  Who knows when you might be expected to know that Carlton from the Fresh Prince hosts Dancing With the Stars?

@Steve L

Oh Steve, some people don't come here to learn.  

They come for a gold star and they want it as quickly as possible.

They resent anything that gets in the way of that.

Steve,
I did the bonus puzzle. It was very easy, despite the fact that I know no more about "reality TV" than what I can't avoid during commercial breaks in other programs or reading the paper or online news. I do feel for people doing themed or tribute puzzles on subjects in which they have no interest.

@Steve L I will just chime in to say that I have done the activity suggested only twice in my life and was almost killed both times (first time at age six when on the way home my dad hit the brakes on an icy road resulting in a crash; second time as a teen when friends decided that since I was a competitive figure skater I would be able to handle the suggested activity at a level higher than the inexperienced ought to attempt).  Despite these unpleasant experiences, I loved Sunday's puzzle, got the them fairly early on, and used to turbocharge the rest of my solving. Did not get the symbols until reading the comments.  My verdict: People complain too much.

TL;DR

If you don't like or identify with the theme, try to enjoy it anyway and don't hate on the constructor.

@Joe
In contemporary American cooking, cornbread usually includes wheat flour--up to 50%--as cornmeal/-flour alone contains no gluten, and doesn't rise. Is this what you make? Bread, or breadlike concoctions, made from cornmeal alone, is called "corn pone" in the South, and "johnnycake" in the North. This is what First Nation peoples ("pone" comes from an Algonquian word) and wheatless European pioneers, would have eaten. But the definitions and distinctions, like the batter, are fluid.

@Bill

I use a cornbread recipe that uses no flour and it rises just fine.  No one calls this recipe pone.

@Bill
I use 1/3 cup AP flour to 1 cup cornmeal (part coarse, part medium grind), with baking powder, a bit of baking soda, buttermilk and an egg.  Not over-mixed; stirred together just before adding 'shortening' as below:
My cast-iron frying pan (no particular 'make'--just the one I've had for 60 years) is heated with  a generous tablespoon of bacon grease, which is combined with the batter swiftly and scooped into the hot pan.  20 minutes at 350F.  
Depending, I might add chopped jalapeños.
I call it "cornbread," but think it's more of a PONE than a 'bread' because it's fairly flat.  
The pan is not used for any other purpose, and I 'scrub' it with coarse salt using a dry paper towel or cloth.
@Bill Celiac here, no problem getting my cornbread to rise!  It’s generally yeast leavening that’s trickier for us.  But cornbread uses baking powder and baking soda.

@MOL
At work, we usually use a mix such as Krusteaz or Jiffy, but I will normally add some sugar (and a couple eggs) to it, to cater to modern American tastes. Although my personal preference would be not sweetened at all.

I've already been excoriated for stating that cornbread made without wheat flour will not rise; perhaps I should have said "will not rise as well, and be denser and more crumbly." And it certainly won't hold its shape as a dough, which is why you have to bake it in a loaf pan or a skillet--although an upturned shovel or gardening hoe will work in a pinch. (And lest you think this a strictly rural American thing, farm implements have been used for cooking in cultures from Hungarian to Japanese.)

I know we've discussed Conrad Richter's *The Awakening Land*--there's a great chapter in it, in which Sayward prepares Tea for her neighbor, Mrs. Covenhoven, accompanied by "*two* different breadstuffs," using only one--the smaller--kettle, as the larger one is being used for soap at the time.

@Nancy Slaps and "spits bars" have been around since at least the 90s, if not earlier. Just because it comes from African American Vernacular English (AAVE) does not mean won't last, because AAVE will still be around even after those terms fall out of popular (non-AAVE-speaker) use. Including AAVE terms in a puzzle is linguistically no different from the occasional answers in Spanish, French, or some other language.
